challenges.question: Day 1: Something to Think About
challenges.solutionSteps: Good job with trying! And if you didn't get the right answer, don't worry, being right or wrong is not the most important thing in learning! These challenges are meant to be strange and hard, and just give you a place to practice thinking of your own ways to solve new problems. Continue on to the Explanation to uncover the math behind the challenge.

challenges.question: Day 2: Your Turn Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: The neat thing is that it doesn't matter which order they eat the slices in, because either way, of the pizza would remain!

challenges.question: Day 2: Your Turn Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps: After Alice eats the pizza, there is of the pizza left. Then Bob eats of what is left... We can cancel factors, leaving us with 1/2. The number of slices left is 3, so half equals three slices, and the whole pizza contains 6 slices.

challenges.question: Day 3: Challenge Explanation 1/4
challenges.solutionSteps: One way to visualize this is that you have a pie cut into slices. The top number means how many slices you take. The bottom number means how many slices are in the whole pie.

challenges.solutionSteps: Let's try to get the denominators close together! If we get their denominators close to 25 or 23, this will make them easier to compare.

challenges.question: Day 3: Challenge Explanation 4/4
challenges.solutionSteps: Notice that the number of miss shots doesn't change, because we are only adding success shots. Since we want 99%, 1% should be miss shots.

challenges.solutionSteps: As you keep adding 2 to the top and 1 to the bottom, after a million times the fraction would be close to 2,000,000/1,000,000 = 2.

challenges.question: Day 4: Something to Think About
challenges.solutionSteps: Good job on trying!...

challenges.question: Day 4: Challenge Explanation 1/4
challenges.solutionSteps: The numerator of the success rate means the number of success shots you made. If you continue to make miss shots, these shouldn't change the numerator!

challenges.question: Day 4: Challenge Explanation 2/4
challenges.solutionSteps: Let's set up our equation. Let x = number of additional shots. Our success rate after is (7+x)/(10+x). To solve, cross-multiply.

challenges.question: Day 4: Challenge Explanation 3/4
challenges.solutionSteps: Let x = total shots in beginning. Success rate was 7/x. After 30 additional success shots, rate was (7+30)/(x+30) which was double.

challenges.question: Day 4: Challenge Explanation 4/4
challenges.solutionSteps: Let x = total shots in beginning. Success rate was 7/x. After 30 shots, rate (7+30)/(x+30) was double. Multiplying both sides...

challenges.solutionSteps: Good job on trying!...

challenges.question: Day 4: Your Turn Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: Let's convert improper fractions into mixed numbers. They all contain 5, so compare the fractional parts! Starting from 1/6, adding one to numerator and denominator gets closer to 1.

challenges.question: Day 4: Your Turn Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps: Let's compare the first fraction in each pair. Notice something funny about the top numbers? The fraction composed by adding numerators and denominators has a value in the middle.

challenges.question: Day 5: Challenge Explanation 1/3
challenges.solutionSteps: The building's shadow is 125 feet long, so the proportions in choice i.) match 2:5. The building plus its antennae give a shadow that is 150 feet long, so choice ii.) also matches. The third choice doesn't match.

challenges.question: Day 5: Challenge Explanation 2/3
challenges.solutionSteps: There are no dimensions given, but without loss of generality, we can call them 2 and 5. Then the shadow is of length 3 and the height will be...

challenges.question: Day 5: Challenge Explanation 3/3
challenges.solutionSteps: The length of the photograph is times its width. The difference in length will be proportional to the difference in width by the same ratio.

challenges.question: Day 5: Your Turn Explanation 1/1
challenges.solutionSteps: In this problem, we are relating distance and amount of gas. We only know how much more gas it takes to travel an additional number of miles.

challenges.question: Day 6: Your Turn Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: From the video, we saw that we should make enough of the recipe to match with the ingredient that has been added by too much.

challenges.question: Day 6: Your Turn Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps: We have as much yellow as we need for one recipe, times as much green, and times as much blue. We should make an amount corresponding to the color we put too much of.

challenges.question: Day 7: Challenge Explanation 4/4
challenges.solutionSteps: A decrease of a number N would turn the number into... An increase of 10% would make 1.1 times its previous value. The 11's and 10's cancel out, leaving N. The number doesn't change! Works for any number - infinite numbers.

challenges.question: Day 7: Your Turn

challenges.question: Day 7: Your Turn Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: The nice thing is that it doesn't matter what our starting number is. The number gets doubled, reduced by 20%, increased by 60%, reduced by P% twice...

challenges.question: Day 7: Your Turn Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps: Initially, Bob and Ann are the same height. Bob's height increases by 10% in one single growth spurt. In the first part, Bob increases by 4% which is also true for Ann.

challenges.question: Day 8: Challenge Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: The important thing to realize is that the commission is not 2.5% of the total price. It is equal to 2.5% of the car price, which is less than $41,000.

challenges.question: Day 8: Challenge Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps: Writing this as a fraction, we get 12.5% = 9/8, so to undo a 12.5% increase, we would need to multiply by 8/9, so we want to decrease by 1/9, which equals 11.11%.

challenges.question: Day 8: Your Turn
challenges.solutionSteps: Good job on trying!...

challenges.question: Day 8: Your Turn Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps: A price increase corresponds to multiplying a number by 5; to undo this, we should multiply by 1/5, which equals 20%.

challenges.question: Day 10: Your Turn Explanation 2/3
challenges.solutionSteps:
Let's use the trick from videos to group together numbers that will multiply to 10.  
The number 2522 is close to 2500; let's pair the 25 with 4 to give 100, multiplied to a factor of 100. 





The 303 and the 31 multiplied together will give 3 × 3 = 9, which is about  10, multiplied to a factor of 1000. 





Lastly, the 2 and the 5016 will give 2 × 5, which is 10, multiplied by a factor of 1000

challenges.question: Day 10: Your Turn Explanation 3/3
challenges.solutionSteps:
The answers are all powers of ten, and so we might guess that approximation (estimating) numbers, a method that we saw in the video, might work. The answers are factors of 10 apart from each other. If we round each number by less than 1%, then the error in our final answer would be far less than this factor of 10.

challenges.question: Day 11: Challenge Explanation 1/3
challenges.solutionSteps:
We are given that the 30-tooth gear spins at 60 revolutions per minute. First, let's ask whether the 45-tooth gear should spin faster or slower. Since it has more teeth, when the 30-tooth gear spins once, the 45-tooth gear will not have finished even one revolution. That means it spins more slowly.

challenges.solutionSteps:
The buckets here are analogous to the gears in the video problem. The kernels of corn are analogous to the teeth on the gears, as they are being added at a constant rate. The 4-pound bucket dumps out more frequently than the 7-pound bucket, and the ratio of number of dumps is the same as the ratios of their sizes.

challenges.solutionSteps:

challenges.question: Day 13: Challenge Explanation 1/2
challenges.solutionSteps:
The y term here should work against the eating rates of the champion and the football players.  Every 10 minutes, y hot dogs are lost, so to compensate, there have to be more football players than before.  This means that the y term should be negative, so the only choices that work are iii.) and iv.)  Choice iv.), 



suggests there is more than one champion, since the champion's rate of eating is

challenges.question: Day 13: Challenge Explanation 2/2
challenges.solutionSteps:
The football players come 6 seconds late, which is one-tenth of one minute, so the football players are only there for  of the time. In the previous situation where the football players were there for the whole time, they could each eat  hot dogs in one minute. Now they each eat  hot dogs in one minute!
